artifacts.content:
Hi Emily,

I just made a cup of tea and thought I’d send a proper update instead of our rushed Sunday check‑ins.

First, I’m still thinking about that awful neuroanatomy midterm back in October 2022 and the “Request for guidance after Neuroanatomy midterm” email you drafted to Dr. Lisa Nguyen at St. Kate’s. I know it felt like the end of the world then, and you were so afraid of being put on academic probation, but look at you now—second year of your OT master’s, juggling classes, clinicals, and that part‑time job at the rehab facility. I’m proud of how you walked through that, even if there were tears and late‑night phone calls from your little apartment in St. Paul.

Honestly, that whole season overlapped with my own messy grief after Grandma Carol died. Around the first anniversary of her passing, in March 2021, I even emailed Pastor Erik—subject line “Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing.” I admitted I was angry at God and questioning half the things I’d grown up saying I believed. It felt scary to put that in writing, but he was kind, and it nudged me toward more hands‑on ways of caring for people instead of just sitting in my head.

That’s part of why I’m leaning more into the senior volunteering now. Talking with my grief counselor, Dr. Karen Olson, at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health here in Minnetonka—those once‑a‑month sessions since November 2023—has helped me see that what steadies me is exactly what you’re training to do: being a calm, consistent presence. I even told her in my March 2024 appointment that watching you stick with OT, even after that neuroanatomy scare, made me want to show up more fully for the older adults I visit.

Day‑to‑day here is pretty ordinary. Dad’s still on the early bus downtown, Buddy still insists on staring at the squirrels from the balcony before his walk, and I’m still reconciling North Star GreenScapes’ accounts in my sweatpants. Grocery prices are ridiculous, but you know that.

Let me know your schedule for the next couple of weekends. If you get a break from rotations, I’d love to drive over to St. Paul, take you to lunch, and restock your freezer with some non‑cafeteria food.

Love you,
Mom

Hi Mark,

I’ve been meaning to write something a little longer than our quick texts. It still bothers me how things blew up last May. When we argued over the phone in May 2024 about how often I should drive to La Crosse to help Aunt Marilyn with her arthritis, and you said I was “checking out now that Carol’s gone,” that really stung. I know you were scared and overwhelmed, but I carried those words around for weeks. When we didn’t talk for almost two months afterward, it felt like I’d lost Mom and my brother at the same time.

Since then I’ve been trying to find a healthier way to be involved with seniors that doesn’t mean constantly feeling like I’m failing someone. Part of that is the grief counseling I’ve been doing with Dr. Karen Olson at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health here in Minnetonka since November 2023—once a month, nothing dramatic, just a space to untangle the guilt and the “should haves” around Mom’s last years.

You remember those last months at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire in 2020. I still think about that medication error—when Mom got the wrong evening dose twice in one week and I finally snapped and wrote that long complaint email to the director on June 12, 2020, copying David and Aunt Marilyn. Those tense meetings afterward left all of us a little mistrustful of senior communities. I know that colors how we’re both thinking about Marilyn now.

All of that is why I’m putting more structure around what I can realistically do. I’m starting regular volunteering with a senior community here in January—Monday, Wednesday, Friday mornings, plus an occasional Saturday event. It lets me be that steady presence without driving back and forth constantly and wearing out my knee.

Speaking of, how is Marilyn doing this week? Has her rheumatologist adjusted anything for the arthritis? I’m looking at my calendar to see when I can come down next—maybe a long weekend in April if the roads behave.

I’d really like us to get back to being on the same team with this, even if we divide things up differently.

Love,
Linda

Hi Pastor Erik,

I wanted to send a quick note of thanks and a bit of an update.

I still remember that email I sent you back in March 2021, with the subject line “Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing.” I was so raw then—angry at God, confused about prayer after watching Carol decline at Oakwood in Eau Claire, and exhausted from all the driving back and forth. Your gentle response (and the way you didn’t seem shocked by my doubts) has stayed with me.

A lot has happened since. You know about the medication mix‑up at Oakwood during Mom’s final months—the wrong evening dose twice in one week, and then my long complaint email to the director on June 12, 2020, with David and Aunt Marilyn copied. Those tense meetings left our whole family wary of senior communities. For a while, every time I walked into a facility I was braced for something to go wrong.

Over the last year and a half, I’ve also been seeing a grief counselor, Dr. Karen Olson, at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health here in Minnetonka—monthly sessions since November 2023. Talking with her has helped me see that my anger and anxiety were really about wanting people like Mom to be treated as whole human beings, not just “beds” or “cases.”

That’s part of why I’m leaning so strongly into the Senior Connections ministry and the new volunteer work I’m starting at a nearby senior community in mid‑January. I keep thinking about June, that retired teacher who played Scrabble with Mom every Wednesday, and how much difference one faithful visitor made. I want to be that kind of presence for someone else’s mother.

Day‑to‑day, life is quieter now that both kids are out of the house. David’s commuting downtown, I’m doing my bookkeeping for North Star GreenScapes mostly from the kitchen table, and Buddy is still shedding on everything. But under the ordinary is this slow, steady sense that God is nudging me toward this work with older adults, even if my theology is still a bit wobbly around the edges.

Thank you again for making room for questions back in 2021. It mattered more than you know.

Peace,
Linda

metadata.subject: An overdue thank‑you and an update

artifacts.content:
Hi David,

Just finished going through the bank feeds and thought I’d jot this down while it’s fresh so we’re on the same page before the weekend.

I was looking again at the savings balance and, whew, it still makes my stomach flutter a little when I think about that $38,000 lump‑sum payment we made on your St. Thomas student loans after we sold the house last October. I know it was the right thing to finally knock out those 2012–2014 project management coursework loans, but having a smaller emergency fund than we’d like is definitely motivating me to keep things simple for the next couple of years. At least the rent here at 2847 Willow Creek Lane is predictable.

That’s part of why I’m committing to the senior volunteering here in Minnetonka instead of anything that would involve travel. I told them I can do about six hours a week, mostly Monday, Wednesday, and Friday mornings, and maybe a Saturday afternoon twice a month if we have at least a week’s notice. We’re staying put in this apartment for the foreseeable future, so it feels safe to promise a full 12 months.

I’m also trying to be realistic about my energy. After that scare with the chest tightness and the stress echocardiogram at Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park last August 2024—even though the results were normal—Dr. Melissa Hart at Allina was pretty clear that I shouldn’t overdo it. She said if my cholesterol numbers aren’t better by spring 2026, we’ll probably have to talk about a statin. Between that and Dr. Olson reminding me not to overschedule myself when we talk at Park Nicollet, mornings feel like the sanest window.

Anyway, can we sit down after dinner on Friday and sketch out the next few months—Emily’s possible visits, Jake’s spring break, and maybe a North Shore weekend in late summer if the car cooperates? I just want to make sure our calendar and our bank account are talking to each other.

Love you,
Linda

Personal planning – volunteering, health, and limits

1. Schedule & energy
- Aim for 6 hrs/week of senior volunteering, mostly Mon/Wed/Fri mornings 9–12.
- Avoid long afternoons on my feet. After the chest tightness scare and stress echocardiogram at Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park in August 2024, Dr. Melissa Hart (Allina) warned that if my cholesterol isn’t better by spring 2026, we’ll likely need to start a statin. She also said not to “overdo long afternoons on your feet,” especially when I’m already tired from bookkeeping.
- Factor in knee limits. Since the cortisone injection in my right knee from Dr. Ravi Patel at Twin Cities Orthopedics (Feb 2025), I’m supposed to avoid repetitive stair‑climbing and standing more than ~2 hours at a stretch. Choose roles that are more sitting/visiting/reading than hauling chairs.

2. Emotional bandwidth
- Monthly grief counseling with Dr. Karen Olson at Park Nicollet Behavioral Health (since Nov 2023) is non‑negotiable. Put “Dr. Olson – grief appt” in the calendar and protect that time.
- Remember: after Mom’s medication error at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire (wrong evening dose twice in one week, complaint email to director on 6/12/2020) and the blow‑up with Mark in May 2024 about how often I was driving to La Crosse for Aunt Marilyn, I am prone to guilt and over‑responsibility.
- The goal is to be a steady, kind presence—not to fix every system or carry every relative.

3. Work boundaries
- North Star GreenScapes bookkeeping: keep it at 20–24 hrs/week.
- Don’t repeat the March 2023 pattern where I stayed up late re‑checking payroll after finding that overtime discrepancy for Crew B and emailing Tom about “Overtime discrepancy – Crew B,” only to be told not to “stir the pot.” Do what’s ethical, document it, then let it go.

4. Self‑care basics
- Gentle yoga 1x/week at community center.
- Daily walks with Buddy, even if short.
- More roasted veggies, fewer creamy casseroles.
- Protect Sunday afternoons for rest/family calls.

Hi Sara,

Catching up between invoices and thought I’d answer your last note before the day gets away from me.

Work at North Star GreenScapes is…fine. Same snow‑plow hours, same muddy receipts. Every now and then I still think about that March 2023 mess when I found the recurring payroll error—overtime hours for one snow‑removal crew weren’t being paid correctly. I sent Tom that email titled “Overtime discrepancy – Crew B,” and he basically told me not to “stir the pot” and to wait until after the busy season. I fixed what I could, but it left me uneasy about the ethics here.

That uneasiness is partly why I’ve leaned so hard into the senior work. You remember Mom’s last months at Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire and the medication error—wrong evening dose twice in one week. I ended up writing a long, detailed complaint email to the director on June 12, 2020, copying David and Aunt Marilyn, and the meetings that followed were so tense. Between that and the argument with Mark in May 2024 over how often I was driving to La Crosse to help Aunt Marilyn with her arthritis (he actually accused me of “checking out now that Carol’s gone”), I realized I needed a healthier, more structured way to be involved.

So, starting mid‑January, I’m doing three regular mornings a week at a senior community here in Minnetonka—mostly one‑on‑one visits, reading aloud, simple games. It feels like a way to channel all that frustration into something good.

On the home front, Emily’s still buried in her OT master’s at St. Kate’s, but doing better since that awful neuroanatomy exam in October 2022 when she emailed her advisor, Dr. Nguyen, “Request for guidance after Neuroanatomy midterm.” Jake’s up in Duluth talking about summer trail work. David’s commuting, Buddy’s shedding, and I’m reminding myself to breathe.

Let me know when you’re free for coffee—maybe the second week of February?

Love,
Linda

Event: Dr. Olson – grief appt
Date: Thursday, March 13, 2025
Time: 10:30 a.m. – 11:20 a.m.
Location: Park Nicollet Behavioral Health, Minnetonka – 3rd floor

Notes:
- Monthly session with grief counselor, Dr. Karen Olson (started Nov 2023). Keep this protected even if North Star work or volunteer schedule gets busy.
- Check‑in topics:
  • Waves of grief around Mom (Carol) still popping up, especially when I’m in senior communities.
  • Revisit the argument with Mark from May 2024 about how often I should drive to La Crosse for Aunt Marilyn and his “checking out now that Carol’s gone” comment—still lingering hurt.
  • Talk about ongoing mistrust of facilities after Oakwood Assisted Living in Eau Claire: medication error in 2020 (wrong evening dose twice in one week), my complaint email to the director on 6/12/2020 with David and Aunt Marilyn copied, and how that colors how I see The Waters and other places.
  • Process anxiety after the chest‑tightness scare and stress echocardiogram at Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park (Aug 2024). Ask how to cope with Dr. Hart’s warning about possibly starting a statin by spring 2026 if cholesterol doesn’t improve.
  • Knee limits after cortisone injection in right knee from Dr. Ravi Patel at Twin Cities Orthopedics (Feb 2025)—frustration about avoiding stairs/standing >2 hours while volunteering.
  • How the March 2021 email to Pastor Erik (“Struggling with faith after Mom’s passing”) still echoes in my head when I’m at church.
  • Sorting out feelings about integrity after North Star’s March 2023 “Overtime discrepancy – Crew B” situation.

Reminders:
- Bring updated list of meds and most recent lab results.
- Leave home by 10:00 a.m. to allow for parking.

Hi everyone,

I hope this email finds you all well! As we start the new year, I wanted to share some exciting news with you. I've decided to start volunteering at The Waters, a wonderful senior living community I've heard so much about. I'll begin on January 15, 2026, and I'm really looking forward to this new chapter.

For those interested, I'll be helping with one-on-one visits and friendly conversations, which is something I've always enjoyed. I love hearing the life stories of older adults and providing companionship, especially for those who feel isolated. I’ll also have the chance to read aloud and assist with letter writing, which reminds me of the times I spent reading to Mom when her eyesight declined. Remember how much she loved those afternoons?

Additionally, I’ll be involved in small group activities like bingo and trivia—similar to what I've done at Grace Lutheran Church. I even get to indulge in some music and reminiscence sessions, which means playing a few hymns on the piano and singing along to classics like ‘Amazing Grace.’

This opportunity combines many of the skills and experiences I've gained over the years. My time volunteering at Grace Lutheran Church and caring for Mom has really prepared me for this. Plus, my work as a bookkeeper gives me a flexible schedule to commit to this consistently. Although I won't be available on Tuesdays, Thursdays, or Sundays, I’m excited to be a regular presence there.

David and I have talked extensively about wanting to create a community where people truly look out for one another as they age. Volunteering at The Waters feels like a step toward that vision. It’s a chance to give back and model the kind of community involvement we want to see more of.

In case of emergencies, David will be my contact—his number is (952) 555-4811. Please keep this in mind as I embark on this new journey.

Looking forward to catching up with everyone soon, whether it’s over a call or a visit. Also, let's plan that road trip along the North Shore this summer—it’s about time we did it!

Love,
Linda
